name: Serbia

colors.symbolism: the blood shed for freedom; pan-Slavic
the sky and the freedom of the Serbian people; pan-Slavic

description: Three equal horizontal stripes — red, blue and white from top to bottom — with the lesser coat of arms placed off-centre toward the hoist, spanning all three stripes.
history: The red-blue-white tricolour has been a Serbian national symbol since the First Serbian Uprising in 1835; after various permutations during the kingdom (1882–1918), Yugoslavia (1918–1991, with the order red-blue-white shared with the Pan-Slavic flag), and the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia / Serbia and Montenegro (1992–2006), the present design (with the lesser coat of arms restored) was adopted by the National Assembly on 11 November 2010, after Serbia became a fully independent state on 5 June 2006.

features.symbolism: the pan-Slavic colours, derived from the Russian tricolour and adopted by Serbia in the 19th century to express Slavic solidarity

features.role: Lesser Coat of Arms of Serbia
features.symbolism: a red shield bearing a white double-headed eagle (recalling Byzantine imperial heritage and the medieval Nemanjić dynasty), surmounted by a gold royal crown, with the small white cross-and-firesteels (the Serbian Cross — the cross with four firesteels representing 'Само слога Србина спасава', 'Only Unity Saves the Serbs') on a red shield on the eagle's breast; the shield is mantled in red and lined ermine, surmounted by the royal crown
